currentUse: Vacant grassed lot on Third Street in Eynon (a village within Archbald borough), retained by the territorial successor Christ the King Parish (Diocese of Scranton) to provide improved access to the adjoining cemetery; no sale of the property has been reported.
summary: The church, known in English-language sources as Saint Mary of Vilna / Our Lady of Vilna, stood on Third Street in Eynon, a village within Archbald borough. It permanently closed in spring 2001 because of toxic mold caused by chronic moisture and drainage problems, with the closure made permanent around 2002, and the building then stood abandoned for over two decades. On March 13, 2024, demolition crews took the building down at the direction of Christ the King Parish, whose pastor Father Ryan Glenn described the demolition as "an act of being a good neighbor" to prevent harm from the deteriorating structure. During demolition, workers removing the cornerstone found a tin time capsule containing a letter dated November 17, 1915, written in Lithuanian, and a photograph believed to be of the pastor at the time; the parish said it was having the letter translated to share with the community. The land is being retained by the parish, planted with grass, and used to improve visitor access to the cemetery — the Our Lady of Vilna cemetery, where the community's Lithuanians were buried, shares fenced grounds with the Polish Our Lady of Czestochowa cemetery. The successor parish itself continues to consolidate: Christ the King Parish (worship sites Saint Thomas Aquinas, Archbald, and Saint Mary of Czestochowa, Eynon) absorbed Sacred Heart of Mary Church in Jermyn effective July 1, 2024. No published follow-up on the translated contents of the 1915 letter was found through mid-2026.

developments.headline: Church permanently closed over toxic mold
developments.detail: The church on Third Street in Eynon closed in spring 2001 due to a mold problem after years of moisture and water-drainage issues; per LRT the closure was made permanent in 2002, and the Diocese of Scranton's consolidated-parishes list records the Eynon 'Our Lady Vilna' church as closed 2002. The building stood abandoned for more than two decades afterward.

sources.title: Letter and photo from 1915 recovered from former Saint Mary of Vilna Church
Demolished Lithuanian church in US unveils 1915 letter to future generations

developments.headline: Building demolished; 1915 Lithuanian time capsule found in cornerstone
developments.detail: After final consultation with parish leaders in 2023, Christ the King Parish had the former Saint Mary of Vilna Church demolished on March 13, 2024 (Stafursky Paving Company completed the work in over four hours). Pastor Father Ryan Glenn called it 'an act of being a good neighbor, making sure this building doesn't cause any harm.' Workers removing the cornerstone found a small tin canister holding a letter dated November 17, 1915, written in Lithuanian, and a photo believed to be of the then-pastor; the letter was well-preserved though torn and discolored in places, and the parish said it was working to translate it and share it with the community.

sources.title: Letter and photo from 1915 recovered from former Saint Mary of Vilna Church
Demolished Lithuanian church in US unveils 1915 letter to future generations

developments.headline: Land retained by parish for cemetery access, not sold
developments.detail: The Diocese of Scranton stated the land will be retained and planted with grass, providing improved access for visitors to the cemetery. Global True Lithuania documents the Our Lady of Vilna cemetery in Eynon, where the parish's Lithuanians were buried, sharing fenced grounds with the Polish Our Lady of Czestochowa cemetery; before demolition, the church's last visible Lithuanian trace was an inscription commemorating pastor Savulis beside an empty statue niche.

sources.title: Letter and photo from 1915 recovered from former Saint Mary of Vilna Church
Pennsylvania Northern Coal Region Lithuanian sites

developments.headline: Successor parish continues consolidating
developments.detail: Christ the King Parish, Archbald — the parish holding the former Lithuanian church's site, with worship sites Saint Thomas Aquinas (Archbald) and Saint Mary of Czestochowa (Eynon) — merged with Sacred Hearts of Jesus and Mary Parish in Jermyn; the diocese's consolidated-parishes list (updated 7/3/2024) records Sacred Heart of Mary Church, Jermyn, joining Christ the King effective 7/1/2024.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: No contradictions on the core facts; several refinements and one notable label conflict in diocesan records. Confirmations: closed spring 2001 with permanent closure ~2002 (matches archive's ~2001-2002 mold/moisture closure); demolished March 2024, refined to the exact date March 13, 2024; 1915 time capsule with Lithuanian letter (dated November 17, 1915) and pastor photo found in the cornerstone, matching the archive note; LRT confirms built 1901, burned 1914, rebuilt 1915. Refinements: (1) the church stood on Third Street in Eynon, a village within Archbald borough — sources place it in 'Eynon' rather than Archbald proper; (2) English-language sources call it 'Saint Mary of Vilna' / 'Our Lady of Vilna,' the anglicization of the Aušros Vartų dedication — no source used 'Immaculate Conception.' Conflict: the Diocese of Scranton's own consolidated-parishes list (7/3/2024) records the Eynon church as 'Our Lady Vilna (Polish) (closed 2002)' — the Polish label contradicts the Lithuanian identity, but LRT, WNEP, Global True Lithuania, and the Lithuanian-language 1915 cornerstone letter all confirm it was the Lithuanian parish (the Polish parish in Eynon was the separate St. Mary of Czestochowa); the diocesan ethnic label appears to be an error. Minor variance: Global True Lithuania says the church was 'constructed in 1914' versus LRT's rebuilt-1915.
gaps: No published follow-up on the translation or contents of the 1915 Lithuanian letter was found (searched Diocese of Scranton, LRT, and general web through July 2026) — the parish said in March 2024 it was translating it to share with the community. The exact canonical closure/suppression date and the parish's formal merger history before 2001 were not found. No sale price or buyer exists to report because the diocese stated the land is being retained by Christ the King Parish. The Times-Tribune's original demolition coverage is paywalled and was not opened directly (its facts reached me via LRT, which cites it); the WNEP article body returned 403/timeouts, so only its headline and search-surfaced summary (Lithuanian church, closed since 2001, mold too costly to repair) were used. No evidence found of preservation campaigns, parishioner reunions, or protests around the 2024 demolition, though absence of coverage is not proof none occurred.
